execution_details.stdout:
# rsyslog configuration file

# For more information see /usr/share/doc/rsyslog-*/rsyslog_conf.html
# or latest version online at http://www.rsyslog.com/doc/rsyslog_conf.html 
# If you experience problems, see http://www.rsyslog.com/doc/troubleshoot.html

#### GLOBAL DIRECTIVES ####

# Where to place auxiliary files
global(workDirectory="/var/lib/rsyslog")

# Use default timestamp format
module(load="builtin:omfile" Template="RSYSLOG_TraditionalFileFormat")

#### MODULES ####

module(load="imuxsock" 	  # provides support for local system logging (e.g. via logger command)
       SysSock.Use="off") # Turn off message reception via local log socket; 
			  # local messages are retrieved through imjournal now.
module(load="imjournal" 	    # provides access to the systemd journal
       UsePid="system" # PID nummber is retrieved as the ID of the process the journal entry originates from
       FileCreateMode="0644" # Set the access permissions for the state file
       StateFile="imjournal.state") # File to store the position in the journal
#module(load="imklog") # reads kernel messages (the same are read from journald)
#module(load="immark") # provides --MARK-- message capability

# Include all config files in /etc/rsyslog.d/
include(file="/etc/rsyslog.d/*.conf" mode="optional")

# Provides UDP syslog reception
# for parameters see http://www.rsyslog.com/doc/imudp.html
#module(load="imudp") # needs to be done just once
#input(type="imudp" port="514")

# Provides TCP syslog reception
# for parameters see http://www.rsyslog.com/doc/imtcp.html
#module(load="imtcp") # needs to be done just once
#input(type="imtcp" port="514")

#### RULES ####

# Log all kernel messages to the console.
# Logging much else clutters up the screen.
#kern.*                                                 /dev/console

# Log anything (except mail) of level info or higher.
# Don't log private authentication messages!
*.info;mail.none;authpriv.none;cron.none                /var/log/messages

# The authpriv file has restricted access.
authpriv.*                                              /var/log/secure

# Log all the mail messages in one place.
mail.*                                                  -/var/log/maillog


# Log cron stuff
cron.*                                                  /var/log/cron

# Everybody gets emergency messages
*.emerg                                                 :omusrmsg:*

# Save news errors of level crit and higher in a special file.
uucp,news.crit                                          /var/log/spooler

# Save boot messages also to boot.log
local7.*                                                /var/log/boot.log


# ### sample forwarding rule ###
#action(type="omfwd"  
# # An on-disk queue is created for this action. If the remote host is
# # down, messages are spooled to disk and sent when it is up again.
#queue.filename="fwdRule1"       # unique name prefix for spool files
#queue.maxdiskspace="1g"         # 1gb space limit (use as much as possible)
#queue.saveonshutdown="on"       # save messages to disk on shutdown
#queue.type="LinkedList"         # run asynchronously
#action.resumeRetryCount="-1"    # infinite retries if host is down
# # Remote Logging (we use TCP for reliable delivery)
# # remote_host is: name/ip, e.g. 192.168.0.1, port optional e.g. 10514
#Target="remote_host" Port="XXX" Protocol="tcp")

The service is **active (running)** and was successfully reloaded after the configuration change.

---

**Configuration file added**

`/etc/rsyslog.d/encrypt.conf` now contains exactly:

```
$ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1
```

File ownership is `root:root` with permissions `0644`.

---

All steps from the remediation plan have been applied, and rsyslog is running with the new directive, satisfying the OpenSCAP rule **rsyslog_encrypt_offload_actionsendstreamdrivermode**. If you wish to perform any further checks (e.g., verify the GnuTLS driver is installed) just let me know.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to propose a plan to ensure rsyslog encrypts off-loaded audit records. According to recommendation, we need to set $ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1 in rsyslog conf. Additionally, need to remove any existing $ActionSendStreamDriverMode entries (to avoid duplicates). Then add the proper line.

We need to inspect /etc/rsyslog.conf and files in /etc/rsyslog.d.

Plan: 1) Verify rsyslog package installed. Use run_cmd to check rpm -q rsyslog. But maybe not needed; assume.

We will read /etc/rsyslog.conf. If line exists (maybe commented), we need to ensure proper value. Use sed to comment removal and set. Simpler: use sed -i to delete any existing $ActionSendStreamDriverMode lines, then add new line at end of rsyslog.conf or in separate file /etc/rsyslog.d/encrypt.conf.

Best to create a new file /etc/rsyslog.d/encrypt.conf with the line. That avoids editing existing file. However need to ensure removal of any other entries to avoid conflict. We'll delete from all .conf files in /etc/rsyslog.d. Use sed -i.

Procedure:
- Ensure /etc/rsyslog.d exists; create if not.
- Remove existing entries: for each file (including /etc/rsyslog.conf) run sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*\$ActionSendStreamDriverMode/d' file.
- Then create /etc/rsyslog.d/encrypt.conf with line: $ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1

We need to read files before modifying.

Steps:
1. read_file /etc/rsyslog.conf
2. read_file /etc/rsyslog.d (list files) maybe read directory? There's no tool for list dir. Could use run_cmd 'ls /etc/rsyslog.d/*.conf' to get list. That's a command; okay.

But we must avoid many commands? We can run sequentially.

Plan detail: Use run_cmd to execute 'rpm -q rsyslog' to check installed, but not required.

We'll propose plan and then submit to review_plan.

We have approval. Need to perform steps. Follow plan: verify rsyslog installed. Then read /etc/rsyslog.conf. Then list files in /etc/rsyslog.d. Remove existing $ActionSendStreamDriverMode lines from main and other conf files.

Then ensure folder exists (mkdir -p). Then write encrypt.conf with line.

Then restart rsyslog (or reload). The feedback mentioned could use reload, but plan originally said restart. Approved plan says restart. We can follow. Use systemctl restart rsyslog.

We must do one command at a time. Use read_file first.

Let's read /etc/rsyslog.conf.

We have no existing .conf files.

Proceed steps:

2. Remove lines (none present). Run sed command on /etc/rsyslog.conf.

3. Ensure /etc/rsyslog.d exists (already exists maybe). Use mkdir -p.

4. Create encrypt.conf with line. Use write_file.

5. Set permissions root:root 0644. Use write_file with mode maybe. The tool write_file can accept mode. We'll set mode "0644".

6. Restart rsyslog: systemctl restart rsyslog.

We have to run each command singly.

First, we could run sed removal: run_cmd sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*\$ActionSendStreamDriverMode/d' /etc/rsyslog.conf

Then mkdir -p /etc/rsyslog.d (maybe already). Then write encrypt.conf.

Then systemctl restart rsyslog.

We might also want to check rsyslog-gnutls package not required per prompt; we can ignore extra suggestions.

Proceed.

We have removed any existing $ActionSendStreamDriverMode lines from rsyslog.conf (none existed). Need to ensure /etc/rsyslog.d exists, create encrypt.conf with correct content, set permissions, restart/reload rsyslog.

We should check if /etc/rsyslog.d exists; it may already exist. Let's list.

We have to add encrypt.conf with the directive, ensure proper permissions. Also maybe need to check rainer script? The review feedback suggests optional improvements but not required. We can proceed with creating file encrypt.conf with line and set correct mode.

We must use write_file. Ensure content ends with newline. Permissions default may be okay but we can set mode 0644. Use write_file with mode "0644". Also reload rsyslog. Use run_cmd "systemctl reload rsyslog". Verify exit code.

Proceed.

We have completed steps: removed old lines, created encrypt.conf with correct content. Need to restart or reload rsyslog. According to feedback, better to reload. Use systemctl reload rsyslog.

Proceed with run_cmd.
